texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The combustion of pulverized coal for power generation results in emissions of various air pollutants that have a detrimental impact on human health and the environment. One of these pollutants is carbon dioxide (CO2), a major greenhouse gas, contributing to climate change. It is well recognized that reducing CO2 emissions from coal-fired power generation can be achieved by two general approaches: improving energy efficiency of the power plant and capturing CO2 from flue gas in end-of-pipe technology. This work focuses on the latter approach, since it is relatively new and
texts.text: The combustion of pulverized coal for power generation results in emissions of various air pollutants that have a detrimental impact on human health and the environment. One of these pollutants is carbon dioxide (CO2), a major greenhouse gas, contributing to climate change. It is well recognized that reducing CO2 emissions from coal-fired power generation can be achieved by two general approaches: improving energy efficiency of the power plant and capturing CO2 from flue gas in end-of-pipe technology. This work focuses on the latter approach, since it is relatively new and

texts.label: footnote
texts.orig: * Corresponding author. Tel.: +1 306 5855665; fax: +1 306 5854855. E-mail address: veawab@uregina.ca (A. Veawab).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: a b s t r a c t
texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This work provides the essential information and approaches for integration of carbon dioxide (CO2) capture units into power plants, particularly the supercritical type, so that energy utilization and CO2 emissions can be well managed in the subject power plants. An in-house model, developed at the University of Regina, Canada, was successfully used for simulating a 500 MW supercritical coal-fired power plant with a post-combustion CO2 capture unit. The simulations enabled sensitivity and parametric study of the net efficiency of the power plant, the coal consumption rate, and the amounts of CO2 captured and avoided. The parameters of interest include CO2 capture efficiency, type of coal, flue gas delivery scheme, type of amine used in the capture unit, and steam pressure supplied to the capture unit for solvent regeneration. The results show that the advancement of MEA-based CO2 capture units through uses of blended monoethanolamine-methyldiethanolamine (MEA-MDEA) and split flow configuration can potentially make the integration of power plant and CO2 capture unit less energy intensive. Despite the increase in energy penalty, it maybeworthcapturing CO2 at a higher efficiency to achieve greater CO2 emissions avoided. Theflue gas delivery scheme and the steam pressure drawn from the power plant to the CO2 capture unit should be considered for process integration.
texts.text: This work provides the essential information and approaches for integration of carbon dioxide (CO2) capture units into power plants, particularly the supercritical type, so that energy utilization and CO2 emissions can be well managed in the subject power plants. An in-house model, developed at the University of Regina, Canada, was successfully used for simulating a 500 MW supercritical coal-fired power plant with a post-combustion CO2 capture unit. The simulations enabled sensitivity and parametric study of the net efficiency of the power plant, the coal consumption rate, and the amounts of CO2 captured and avoided. The parameters of interest include CO2 capture efficiency, type of coal, flue gas delivery scheme, type of amine used in the capture unit, and steam pressure supplied to the capture unit for solvent regeneration. The results show that the advancement of MEA-based CO2 capture units through uses of blended monoethanolamine-methyldiethanolamine (MEA-MDEA) and split flow configuration can potentially make the integration of power plant and CO2 capture unit less energy intensive. Despite the increase in energy penalty, it maybeworthcapturing CO2 at a higher efficiency to achieve greater CO2 emissions avoided. Theflue gas delivery scheme and the steam pressure drawn from the power plant to the CO2 capture unit should be considered for process integration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Capturing CO2 from flue gas streams requires cost-effective capture technology and energy-efficient integration of the capture unit into the power plant. By far, amine-based absorption is the best available technology for CO2 capture from low-pressure and large volume gas streams. The costs of this process derive largely from high-energy consumption during solvent regeneration. To put the absorption process into operation, a certain quantity of energy must be withdrawn from the steam cycle to use in the CO2 capture operation, as shown in Fig. 1. This causes a reduction in net
texts.text: Capturing CO2 from flue gas streams requires cost-effective capture technology and energy-efficient integration of the capture unit into the power plant. By far, amine-based absorption is the best available technology for CO2 capture from low-pressure and large volume gas streams. The costs of this process derive largely from high-energy consumption during solvent regeneration. To put the absorption process into operation, a certain quantity of energy must be withdrawn from the steam cycle to use in the CO2 capture operation, as shown in Fig. 1. This causes a reduction in net

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This work provides comprehensive understanding of how the CO2 capture unit should be integrated into the supercritical power plant so that the energy utilization and CO2 emissions can be managed economically and efficiently. Such understanding was derived from the results of sensitivity and parametric study of the net efficiency of the power plant, coal consumption rate, and amounts of CO2 captured and avoided. TheparametersofinterestincludeCO2 captureefficiency, type of coal, flue gas delivery scheme, type of amine used in the capture unit, and steam pressure supplied to the capture unit for solvent regeneration.
texts.text: This work provides comprehensive understanding of how the CO2 capture unit should be integrated into the supercritical power plant so that the energy utilization and CO2 emissions can be managed economically and efficiently. Such understanding was derived from the results of sensitivity and parametric study of the net efficiency of the power plant, coal consumption rate, and amounts of CO2 captured and avoided. TheparametersofinterestincludeCO2 captureefficiency, type of coal, flue gas delivery scheme, type of amine used in the capture unit, and steam pressure supplied to the capture unit for solvent regeneration.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Methodology
texts.text: 2. Methodology

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The sensitivity and parametric study was carried out by simulation using an integrated power plant/gas absorption model developed at the University of Regina, Canada. As illustrated in Fig. 2, the integrated model consists of two working modules: power-cycle and CO2 capture. The powercycle module comprises a series of interconnected sub-models of process components including a coal-fired boiler, an air preheater, steam turbines ( i . e . high-pressure, HP; intermediate pressure, IP; low-pressure, LP), a series of feed water heaters (FWHs), a low-pressure condenser, and boiler feed water pumps. The power-cycle module was built on the principles of coal combustion, combustion chemistry, heat transfer from combustion zone to the generated flue gas and steam, and thermodynamicsofsteampower-cycle. As such,simulationof this module provides essential information on rate of coal consumption, thermal efficiency, net plant efficiency and electricity output. More importantly, the simulation also provides the quality and quantity of steam throughout the power-cycle as well as the emission rate, temperature, and composition of the flue gas. Such information is extremely important for the integration of the CO2 capture unit into the power plant.
texts.text: The sensitivity and parametric study was carried out by simulation using an integrated power plant/gas absorption model developed at the University of Regina, Canada. As illustrated in Fig. 2, the integrated model consists of two working modules: power-cycle and CO2 capture. The powercycle module comprises a series of interconnected sub-models of process components including a coal-fired boiler, an air preheater, steam turbines ( i . e . high-pressure, HP; intermediate pressure, IP; low-pressure, LP), a series of feed water heaters (FWHs), a low-pressure condenser, and boiler feed water pumps. The power-cycle module was built on the principles of coal combustion, combustion chemistry, heat transfer from combustion zone to the generated flue gas and steam, and thermodynamicsofsteampower-cycle. As such,simulationof this module provides essential information on rate of coal consumption, thermal efficiency, net plant efficiency and electricity output. More importantly, the simulation also provides the quality and quantity of steam throughout the power-cycle as well as the emission rate, temperature, and composition of the flue gas. Such information is extremely important for the integration of the CO2 capture unit into the power plant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CO2 capture module, the second component of the integrated model, was developed specifically for evaluating the performance of CO2 absorption processes using singleand blended-amine solutions. Specifics of the design and development of this CO2 capture module can be found in Aroonwilas et al. (2003). In terms of general description, the capture module is a mechanistic type capable of producing the design specifications of the entire absorption process, including gas absorber and solvent regenerator, as well as the operational information of the process, such as reboiler heat duty and consumption of other process utilities (electricity and cooling water). The module was built from knowledge of kinetics and thermodynamics of CO2 absorption into aqueous amine solutions and also of hydrodynamic features of gasliquid contactors commonly used in the process ( i . e . packed and tray columns). The module was based on an integration of several sub-models: (1) mechanism of liquid flow distribution, (2) mass-transfer process with chemical reactions, and (3) vapour-liquid equilibrium (VLE). The liquid distribution submodel (LDM) was used to evaluate the distribution features of irrigating liquid, at different locations inside the gas-liquid contactors, during the absorption and regeneration operation. The liquid distribution information was then used to calculate the interfacial area. The mass-transfer sub-model was associated with the use of two-film theory for evaluating the overall mass-transfer coefficient ( K G) across the gas-liquid boundary. To calculate the individual mass-transfer coefficient, penetration theory was incorporated into this submodel,togetherwiththeknowledgeofabsorptionkinetics and the enhancement factor equation. The equilibrium behaviour was evaluated using the electrolyte non-random two liquid (NRTL) model. The model simulation was based on a theoretical column design procedure for adiabatic gas absorption with chemical reaction, which accounted for heat of reaction, solvent (or water) evaporation and condensation, chemical reaction in liquid phase, and simultaneous heat- and mass-transfer processes. The simulation of the absorption and regeneration columns was achieved by dividing the column height into a number of sections. Each of these sections was treated as a non-equilibrium (or rate-based) discrete stage, governed by material and energy equations.
texts.text: The CO2 capture module, the second component of the integrated model, was developed specifically for evaluating the performance of CO2 absorption processes using singleand blended-amine solutions. Specifics of the design and development of this CO2 capture module can be found in Aroonwilas et al. (2003). In terms of general description, the capture module is a mechanistic type capable of producing the design specifications of the entire absorption process, including gas absorber and solvent regenerator, as well as the operational information of the process, such as reboiler heat duty and consumption of other process utilities (electricity and cooling water). The module was built from knowledge of kinetics and thermodynamics of CO2 absorption into aqueous amine solutions and also of hydrodynamic features of gasliquid contactors commonly used in the process ( i . e . packed and tray columns). The module was based on an integration of several sub-models: (1) mechanism of liquid flow distribution, (2) mass-transfer process with chemical reactions, and (3) vapour-liquid equilibrium (VLE). The liquid distribution submodel (LDM) was used to evaluate the distribution features of irrigating liquid, at different locations inside the gas-liquid contactors, during the absorption and regeneration operation. The liquid distribution information was then used to calculate the interfacial area. The mass-transfer sub-model was associated with the use of two-film theory for evaluating the overall mass-transfer coefficient ( K G) across the gas-liquid boundary. To calculate the individual mass-transfer coefficient, penetration theory was incorporated into this submodel,togetherwiththeknowledgeofabsorptionkinetics and the enhancement factor equation. The equilibrium behaviour was evaluated using the electrolyte non-random two liquid (NRTL) model. The model simulation was based on a theoretical column design procedure for adiabatic gas absorption with chemical reaction, which accounted for heat of reaction, solvent (or water) evaporation and condensation, chemical reaction in liquid phase, and simultaneous heat- and mass-transfer processes. The simulation of the absorption and regeneration columns was achieved by dividing the column height into a number of sections. Each of these sections was treated as a non-equilibrium (or rate-based) discrete stage, governed by material and energy equations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Integration of the CO2 capture unit was evaluated by simulating the two modules simultaneously. Generally, composition and flow rate of the flue gas generated from the power-cycle module were utilized as the primary input in the capture module for evaluating the net CO2 emission rate and energy requirement for CO2 capture. Such output information was subsequently sent back to the power-cycle module for final analysis of energy penalty. Note that the lowpressure steam from power-cycle was used, as the energy source for the reboiler in the absorption process, while a small fraction of electricity produced from the cycle was the source for other process components, such as pumps and blowers.
texts.text: Integration of the CO2 capture unit was evaluated by simulating the two modules simultaneously. Generally, composition and flow rate of the flue gas generated from the power-cycle module were utilized as the primary input in the capture module for evaluating the net CO2 emission rate and energy requirement for CO2 capture. Such output information was subsequently sent back to the power-cycle module for final analysis of energy penalty. Note that the lowpressure steam from power-cycle was used, as the energy source for the reboiler in the absorption process, while a small fraction of electricity produced from the cycle was the source for other process components, such as pumps and blowers.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Reference power plant
texts.text: 3.1. Reference power plant

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A 500 MW net supercritical coal-fired power plant without a CO2 capture unit was used as the reference plant in this study.
texts.text: A 500 MW net supercritical coal-fired power plant without a CO2 capture unit was used as the reference plant in this study.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The power plant was configured to provide a main steam at 28.5 MPa/600 8 C, a reheated steam at 6.0 MPa/600 8 C, and a low-pressure turbine backpressure (or condenser pressure) of 5 kPa. A series of feed water heaters was considered for improved plant efficiency. The schematic flow diagram of the reference power plant is shown in Fig. 4.
texts.text: The power plant was configured to provide a main steam at 28.5 MPa/600 8 C, a reheated steam at 6.0 MPa/600 8 C, and a low-pressure turbine backpressure (or condenser pressure) of 5 kPa. A series of feed water heaters was considered for improved plant efficiency. The schematic flow diagram of the reference power plant is shown in Fig. 4.

texts.text: 3.2. CO2 capture unit with a split flow scheme

texts.orig: Fig. 5 illustrates two process configurations of a CO2 capture unit ( i . e . conventional and split flow of CO2-riched solution schemes). The split flow scheme was developed as a modified configuration of the conventional scheme to reduce steam consumption and cost of CO2 capture (Kohl and Nielsen, 1997). Its key feature is a division of rich-solution from the absorber

The split flow scheme was developed as a modified configuration of the conventional scheme to reduce steam consumption and cost of CO2 capture (Kohl and Nielsen, 1997). Its key feature is a division of rich-solution from the absorber

texts.label: caption

texts.text: Fig. 5 - Process flow diagrams of: (a) conventional amine process and (b) split flow process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: into two streams. The first stream enters the top of the regenerator, flows downward, leaves the regenerator from the midpoint, and enters the absorber at the midpoint. The second stream enters the regenerator at the midpoint, flows downward, leaves the regenerator from the bottom, and eventually enters the absorber at the top. Aroonwilas and Veawab (2006a) reported that the split flow scheme in Fig. 5 requires reboiler heat duty as low as 2900 kJ/kg CO2 with a 95% CO2 capture efficiency and a large CO2 cyclic capacity of solution. However, the regenerator height is increased to accommodate the CO2 stripping. A trade-off between the reduction in utility cost and the increase in capital cost plays an essential role in process optimization to achieve the lowest cost of CO2 capture.
texts.text: into two streams. The first stream enters the top of the regenerator, flows downward, leaves the regenerator from the midpoint, and enters the absorber at the midpoint. The second stream enters the regenerator at the midpoint, flows downward, leaves the regenerator from the bottom, and eventually enters the absorber at the top. Aroonwilas and Veawab (2006a) reported that the split flow scheme in Fig. 5 requires reboiler heat duty as low as 2900 kJ/kg CO2 with a 95% CO2 capture efficiency and a large CO2 cyclic capacity of solution. However, the regenerator height is increased to accommodate the CO2 stripping. A trade-off between the reduction in utility cost and the increase in capital cost plays an essential role in process optimization to achieve the lowest cost of CO2 capture.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3. CO2 capture unit using MEA and blended MEA-MDEA solvents
texts.text: 3.3. CO2 capture unit using MEA and blended MEA-MDEA solvents

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This work includes both single-MEA and blended MEA-MDEA in the simulation of the integration of the CO2 capture unit into the power plant. MEA-MDEA is a potential solvent that can replace the single-MEA because it combines favourable features of MDEA ( i . e . low energy requirement and high CO2 absorption capacity) together with a fast CO2 absorption rate of MEA, thereby presenting a cost-saving opportunity for CO2 capture (Aroonwilas and Veawab, 2006b). The cost saving is primarily attributed to the trade-off feature of the energy consumption during solvent regeneration and the CO2 capture performance. The blended solvent typically requires less energy for solvent regeneration compared to MEA, reflecting a lower steam cost, but their CO2 capture performance is lower, reflecting a larger capital investment. The favourable saving is achieved when the MEA-MDEA process is operated under appropriate mixing ratio and operating conditions.
texts.text: This work includes both single-MEA and blended MEA-MDEA in the simulation of the integration of the CO2 capture unit into the power plant. MEA-MDEA is a potential solvent that can replace the single-MEA because it combines favourable features of MDEA ( i . e . low energy requirement and high CO2 absorption capacity) together with a fast CO2 absorption rate of MEA, thereby presenting a cost-saving opportunity for CO2 capture (Aroonwilas and Veawab, 2006b). The cost saving is primarily attributed to the trade-off feature of the energy consumption during solvent regeneration and the CO2 capture performance. The blended solvent typically requires less energy for solvent regeneration compared to MEA, reflecting a lower steam cost, but their CO2 capture performance is lower, reflecting a larger capital investment. The favourable saving is achieved when the MEA-MDEA process is operated under appropriate mixing ratio and operating conditions.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Results and discussion
texts.text: 4. Results and discussion

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1. Reference power plant without CO2 capture unit
texts.text: 4.1. Reference power plant without CO2 capture unit

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Table 1 shows simulation results of three reference power plants using three different types of coal ( i . e . lignite, subbituminous, and bituminous). Among these coals, bituminous is the most preferable, while lignite is the least preferable, due to their respective contributions to plant efficiency, coal consumption, and amount of CO2 produced in the combustion process. For a constant net output, bituminous yields the highest plant efficiency, the least coal consumption rate, and consequently the least CO2 emission rate. Lignite, on the other hand, leads to the opposite trends. This is mainly attributed to high moisture content (33%) in lignite compared to 23% and 5.2% in sub-bituminous and bituminous, respectively.
texts.text: Table 1 shows simulation results of three reference power plants using three different types of coal ( i . e . lignite, subbituminous, and bituminous). Among these coals, bituminous is the most preferable, while lignite is the least preferable, due to their respective contributions to plant efficiency, coal consumption, and amount of CO2 produced in the combustion process. For a constant net output, bituminous yields the highest plant efficiency, the least coal consumption rate, and consequently the least CO2 emission rate. Lignite, on the other hand, leads to the opposite trends. This is mainly attributed to high moisture content (33%) in lignite compared to 23% and 5.2% in sub-bituminous and bituminous, respectively.

texts.orig: 4.2. Power plant with CO2 capture unit
texts.text: 4.2. Power plant with CO2 capture unit

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2.1. CO2 capture technology
texts.text: 4.2.1. CO2 capture technology

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The supercritical power plant with CO2 capture unit was simulated in two different modes, constant net output of the power plant and constant gross plant size. The first mode requires an increase in the gross plant size with CO2 capture to maintain a net power output of 500 MW, while the second mode requires the reference power plant to be derated by the energy requirement for CO2 capture activities. In both modes, four types of CO2 capture technologies were considered, single MEA with conventional process configuration, single MEA
texts.text: The supercritical power plant with CO2 capture unit was simulated in two different modes, constant net output of the power plant and constant gross plant size. The first mode requires an increase in the gross plant size with CO2 capture to maintain a net power output of 500 MW, while the second mode requires the reference power plant to be derated by the energy requirement for CO2 capture activities. In both modes, four types of CO2 capture technologies were considered, single MEA with conventional process configuration, single MEA

texts.orig: i n t e r n a t i o n a l j o u r n a l of greenhouse gas control 1 (2007) 143-150
texts.text: i n t e r n a t i o n a l j o u r n a l of greenhouse gas control 1 (2007) 143-150

texts.label: text
texts.orig: with advanced or split flow configuration, and blended MEAMDEA formulas 1 and 2 with conventional configuration.
texts.text: with advanced or split flow configuration, and blended MEAMDEA formulas 1 and 2 with conventional configuration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The results in Table 2 show that by installing the CO2 capture unit using conventional MEA, the net efficiency of the powerplant is reduced from 46.5 to as low as 36.8%, causing an energy penalty of approximately a 10% point drop or 26% net output. Such energy penalty is attributed to the steam consumption during solvent regeneration in the CO2 capture unit ( i . e . the fraction of low-pressure steam in the power-cycle that is extracted and delivered to the reboiler rather than to the LP turbine for electricity generation). The CO2 emissions after the application of the CO2 capture unit are, thus, reduced from 700 to only 88.5 kg/MW h, and the CO2 emissions avoided are 760 tonnes per day per percent point drop of energy penalty. As a result, the power plant is required to increase its gross output by as much as 26% to maintain a constant net power output of 500 MW.
texts.text: The results in Table 2 show that by installing the CO2 capture unit using conventional MEA, the net efficiency of the powerplant is reduced from 46.5 to as low as 36.8%, causing an energy penalty of approximately a 10% point drop or 26% net output. Such energy penalty is attributed to the steam consumption during solvent regeneration in the CO2 capture unit ( i . e . the fraction of low-pressure steam in the power-cycle that is extracted and delivered to the reboiler rather than to the LP turbine for electricity generation). The CO2 emissions after the application of the CO2 capture unit are, thus, reduced from 700 to only 88.5 kg/MW h, and the CO2 emissions avoided are 760 tonnes per day per percent point drop of energy penalty. As a result, the power plant is required to increase its gross output by as much as 26% to maintain a constant net power output of 500 MW.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Comparedto the conventional MEA unit, the MEA unit with the advanced split flow configuration offers a higher net plant efficiency of 40.1% and, thus, a lower energy penalty of a 6.4% point drop or 16% net output. With this energy penalty, the advanced MEA unit is required to increase its gross output by 16%, less than that in the conventional MEA unit, to maintain the constant net power output. Consequently, less CO2 emissions are generated. This indicates that the split flow configuration reduces the energy penalty and increases the CO2 avoided per energy penalty.
texts.text: Comparedto the conventional MEA unit, the MEA unit with the advanced split flow configuration offers a higher net plant efficiency of 40.1% and, thus, a lower energy penalty of a 6.4% point drop or 16% net output. With this energy penalty, the advanced MEA unit is required to increase its gross output by 16%, less than that in the conventional MEA unit, to maintain the constant net power output. Consequently, less CO2 emissions are generated. This indicates that the split flow configuration reduces the energy penalty and increases the CO2 avoided per energy penalty.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addition to the split flow configuration, a blended MEAMDEA based CO2 capture unit also offers advantages over the conventional MEA unit. Its net plant efficiency can be reduced from that of the MEA unit by a 2.6-4.8% point, depending on operating conditions. Such reduction can be translated into energy penalty ranging from 6 to 12% and a much higher CO2 emissions avoided per energy penalty compared to the MEA unit.
texts.text: In addition to the split flow configuration, a blended MEAMDEA based CO2 capture unit also offers advantages over the conventional MEA unit. Its net plant efficiency can be reduced from that of the MEA unit by a 2.6-4.8% point, depending on operating conditions. Such reduction can be translated into energy penalty ranging from 6 to 12% and a much higher CO2 emissions avoided per energy penalty compared to the MEA unit.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With the above information, the advancement of MEA technology through uses of MEA-MDEA solvent and split flow configuration appears to provide a promising opportunity in making the integration of power plant and CO2 capture unit less energy intensive and more environmentally beneficial. As seen from Figs. 6 and 7, the MEA-MDEA unit (the best case)
texts.text: With the above information, the advancement of MEA technology through uses of MEA-MDEA solvent and split flow configuration appears to provide a promising opportunity in making the integration of power plant and CO2 capture unit less energy intensive and more environmentally beneficial. As seen from Figs. 6 and 7, the MEA-MDEA unit (the best case)

texts.orig: i n t e r n a t i o n a l j o u r n a l of greenhouse gas control 1 (2007) 143-150

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 7 - Relationship between CO2 emission avoided and net efficiency of power plant (CO2 removal efficiency of 90%).
texts.text: Fig. 7 - Relationship between CO2 emission avoided and net efficiency of power plant (CO2 removal efficiency of 90%).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: yields higher net plant efficiency, less energy penalty, and higher CO2 emissions avoided than the split flow and, in turn, the conventional MEA units. Such advantages of the MEAMDEA unit are primarily due to the energy requirement for solvent regeneration. According to our simulation results, the conventional MEA requires approximately 4.8 MJ/kg CO2 (90,000 Btu/lb mol CO2 at lean CO2 loading of 0.18 mol/mol and 5 kmol/m 3 MEA solution) to strip CO2 during solvent regeneration while the Split Flow requires approximately 3.2 MJ/kg CO2 (60,000 Btu/lb mol CO2 at lean CO2 loading of 0.21 mol/mol and 5 kmol/m 3 MEA solution). The MEA-MDEA requires the least energy, ranging from 1.2 to 2.4 MJ/kg CO2, depending upon its mixing ratio (MEA:MDEA mole ratio of up to 2:1) and operating condition (lean CO2 loading ranging from 0.10 to 0.22 mol/mol). The low energy requirement for the blended MEA-MDEA solution used in this study is supported by the values of reboiler heat duty obtained experimentally by Sakwattanapong et al. (2005).
texts.text: yields higher net plant efficiency, less energy penalty, and higher CO2 emissions avoided than the split flow and, in turn, the conventional MEA units. Such advantages of the MEAMDEA unit are primarily due to the energy requirement for solvent regeneration. According to our simulation results, the conventional MEA requires approximately 4.8 MJ/kg CO2 (90,000 Btu/lb mol CO2 at lean CO2 loading of 0.18 mol/mol and 5 kmol/m 3 MEA solution) to strip CO2 during solvent regeneration while the Split Flow requires approximately 3.2 MJ/kg CO2 (60,000 Btu/lb mol CO2 at lean CO2 loading of 0.21 mol/mol and 5 kmol/m 3 MEA solution). The MEA-MDEA requires the least energy, ranging from 1.2 to 2.4 MJ/kg CO2, depending upon its mixing ratio (MEA:MDEA mole ratio of up to 2:1) and operating condition (lean CO2 loading ranging from 0.10 to 0.22 mol/mol). The low energy requirement for the blended MEA-MDEA solution used in this study is supported by the values of reboiler heat duty obtained experimentally by Sakwattanapong et al. (2005).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It should be noted here that the above discussions are based on energy aspect, not process cost. Uses of MEA-MDEA and split flow may require more capital expenditures due to a taller absorber and regenerator, respectively. For instance, as reported in previous literature (Aroonwilas and Veawab, 2004), MEA-MDEA solutions having slower rates of absorption with CO2 would require absorber heights of more than three times the height for MEA solution in order to achieve an identical CO2 removal target from flue gas. The higher column height and the lower energy requirement for MEA-MDEA solution present a trade-off effect between capital and operating costs associated with operation of a CO2 capture unit.
texts.text: It should be noted here that the above discussions are based on energy aspect, not process cost. Uses of MEA-MDEA and split flow may require more capital expenditures due to a taller absorber and regenerator, respectively. For instance, as reported in previous literature (Aroonwilas and Veawab, 2004), MEA-MDEA solutions having slower rates of absorption with CO2 would require absorber heights of more than three times the height for MEA solution in order to achieve an identical CO2 removal target from flue gas. The higher column height and the lower energy requirement for MEA-MDEA solution present a trade-off effect between capital and operating costs associated with operation of a CO2 capture unit.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2.2. CO2 capture efficiency
texts.text: 4.2.2. CO2 capture efficiency

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CO2 capture efficiency is an important parameter for the integration of a CO2 capture unit into a power plant, since it relates directly to the net plant efficiency and energy penalty. As seen from Fig. 6, higher CO2 capture efficiency causes the net efficiency of the power plant to decrease and the energy penalty to increase. This can be explained by the fact that a higher amount of energy is required at the reboiler to achieve a greater CO2 stripping efficiency and CO2 cyclic capacity of solution, which provides a greater mass-transfer driving force for CO2 absorption.
texts.text: CO2 capture efficiency is an important parameter for the integration of a CO2 capture unit into a power plant, since it relates directly to the net plant efficiency and energy penalty. As seen from Fig. 6, higher CO2 capture efficiency causes the net efficiency of the power plant to decrease and the energy penalty to increase. This can be explained by the fact that a higher amount of energy is required at the reboiler to achieve a greater CO2 stripping efficiency and CO2 cyclic capacity of solution, which provides a greater mass-transfer driving force for CO2 absorption.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 8 - Effect of CO2 capture efficiency on environmental benefit in terms of CO2 emission avoided.
texts.text: Fig. 8 - Effect of CO2 capture efficiency on environmental benefit in terms of CO2 emission avoided.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CO2 capture efficiency also relates to CO2 emissions avoided per energy penalty. That is, its increase leads to an increase in CO2 emissions avoided per energy penalty (Fig. 8). Due to this relationship, it may be worth capturing CO2 at a higher efficiency to receive greater environmental benefit or CO2 emissions avoided, in spite of the increase in energy penalty.
texts.text: The CO2 capture efficiency also relates to CO2 emissions avoided per energy penalty. That is, its increase leads to an increase in CO2 emissions avoided per energy penalty (Fig. 8). Due to this relationship, it may be worth capturing CO2 at a higher efficiency to receive greater environmental benefit or CO2 emissions avoided, in spite of the increase in energy penalty.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2.3. Coal type
texts.text: 4.2.3. Coal type

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Coal type demonstrates certain impacts on power plant performance and CO2 emissions avoided. Fig. 8 shows that the bituminous (Pittsburg #8) generally offers about 2% higher net power efficiency and 20% less coal consumption rate compared to the lignite from North Dakota. The lignite, however, offers higher environmental benefit in terms of CO2 avoided per unit of energy penalty. The higher amount of CO2 avoided per energy penalty essentially results from the higher CO2 emission rate produced during combustion processes using lignite.
texts.text: Coal type demonstrates certain impacts on power plant performance and CO2 emissions avoided. Fig. 8 shows that the bituminous (Pittsburg #8) generally offers about 2% higher net power efficiency and 20% less coal consumption rate compared to the lignite from North Dakota. The lignite, however, offers higher environmental benefit in terms of CO2 avoided per unit of energy penalty. The higher amount of CO2 avoided per energy penalty essentially results from the higher CO2 emission rate produced during combustion processes using lignite.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2.4. Flue gas delivery scheme
texts.text: 4.2.4. Flue gas delivery scheme

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Twoflue gas delivery schemes were evaluated in this work. As seen from Fig. 9, the first scheme involves a delivery of the full stream of flue gas to the CO2 capture unit, whereas the second
texts.text: Twoflue gas delivery schemes were evaluated in this work. As seen from Fig. 9, the first scheme involves a delivery of the full stream of flue gas to the CO2 capture unit, whereas the second

texts.orig: Fig. 10 - Effect of steam pressure and amine type on net efficiency of power plant.
texts.text: Fig. 10 - Effect of steam pressure and amine type on net efficiency of power plant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: scheme involves a delivery of only a partial stream of flue gas. The simulation results indicate that both delivery schemes result in a similar energy penalty for capture when the CO2 capture efficiency is greater than 70%. Below this capture efficiency, partial flue gas delivery offers lower energy penalty. This is simply because the partial flow scheme requires less energy for driving the flue gas blowers located upstream of the capture unit. This finding suggests that the flue gas delivery scheme should be considered as an important process integration criterion, especially when only a fraction of gross CO2 emission is set as the power plant removal target.
texts.text: scheme involves a delivery of only a partial stream of flue gas. The simulation results indicate that both delivery schemes result in a similar energy penalty for capture when the CO2 capture efficiency is greater than 70%. Below this capture efficiency, partial flue gas delivery offers lower energy penalty. This is simply because the partial flow scheme requires less energy for driving the flue gas blowers located upstream of the capture unit. This finding suggests that the flue gas delivery scheme should be considered as an important process integration criterion, especially when only a fraction of gross CO2 emission is set as the power plant removal target.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2.5. Steam pressure supplied to CO2 capture unit
texts.text: 4.2.5. Steam pressure supplied to CO2 capture unit

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The steam pressure drawn from the power plant and supplied to the CO2 capture unit for solvent regeneration has an effect on the net efficiency of the power plant and on the energy penalty. From Fig. 10, drawing lower steam pressure to the CO2 capture unit results in greater net plant efficiency and lower energy penalty. The net power efficiency can increase from 39 to 41% when steam pressure of 200 kPa is used instead of the 400 kPa used for MEA regeneration.
texts.text: The steam pressure drawn from the power plant and supplied to the CO2 capture unit for solvent regeneration has an effect on the net efficiency of the power plant and on the energy penalty. From Fig. 10, drawing lower steam pressure to the CO2 capture unit results in greater net plant efficiency and lower energy penalty. The net power efficiency can increase from 39 to 41% when steam pressure of 200 kPa is used instead of the 400 kPa used for MEA regeneration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It should be noted that the steam pressure drawn to the CO2 capture unit must supply sufficient temperature gradients for heat-transfer in the reboiler. Based on our simulation, a steam pressure of 200 kPa is adequate for MEA-MDEA but may not be adequate for MEA processes. For instance, based on the temperature approach of 10 8 C for reboiler and a reboiler heat duty of 3100 kJ/kg CO2, operating a reboiler in the MEA unit using 200 kPa instead of 300 kPa causes the reboiler temperature to reduce from 120 to 110 8 C, resulting in an increase in lean CO2 loading from 0.20 to 0.24 mol/mol. Such levels of lean loading may not be sufficient to achieve high CO2 capture efficiency in the MEA process.
texts.text: It should be noted that the steam pressure drawn to the CO2 capture unit must supply sufficient temperature gradients for heat-transfer in the reboiler. Based on our simulation, a steam pressure of 200 kPa is adequate for MEA-MDEA but may not be adequate for MEA processes. For instance, based on the temperature approach of 10 8 C for reboiler and a reboiler heat duty of 3100 kJ/kg CO2, operating a reboiler in the MEA unit using 200 kPa instead of 300 kPa causes the reboiler temperature to reduce from 120 to 110 8 C, resulting in an increase in lean CO2 loading from 0.20 to 0.24 mol/mol. Such levels of lean loading may not be sufficient to achieve high CO2 capture efficiency in the MEA process.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusion
texts.text: 5. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Integration of a CO2 capture unit into a supercritical power plant can be implemented to achieve minimum energy penalty for CO2 capture and maximum CO2 emission avoided by: (1) using advanced amine technologies, such as MEAMDEA solvent and split-flow configuration; (2) drawing a suitable steam pressure to the CO2 capture unit ( e . g . 200 kPa for MEA-MDEA regeneration); (3) operating the CO2 capture unit at a high removal efficiency to obtain an acceptable value of CO2 avoided per energy penalty; (4) choosing a flue gas delivery scheme that is suitable for the CO2 capture efficiency.
texts.text: Integration of a CO2 capture unit into a supercritical power plant can be implemented to achieve minimum energy penalty for CO2 capture and maximum CO2 emission avoided by: (1) using advanced amine technologies, such as MEAMDEA solvent and split-flow configuration; (2) drawing a suitable steam pressure to the CO2 capture unit ( e . g . 200 kPa for MEA-MDEA regeneration); (3) operating the CO2 capture unit at a high removal efficiency to obtain an acceptable value of CO2 avoided per energy penalty; (4) choosing a flue gas delivery scheme that is suitable for the CO2 capture efficiency.
